summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Volcanoes & Hot Spots
summary_sections.content_html:
Volcanoes form where magma reaches Earth's surface. They are associated with convergent boundaries, divergent boundaries, and hot spots.

  • Types of volcanoes:
    • Shield volcanoes: broad, gently sloping; built by low-viscosity basaltic lava flows (e.g., Mauna Loa)
    • Cinder cone: small, steep; built from ejected lava fragments
    • Composite (stratovolcano): tall, steep; alternating layers of lava and ash; explosive eruptions (e.g., Mount St. Helens)
  • Hot spots: volcanic areas over unusually hot zones in the mantle, not at plate boundaries. Plates move over the stationary hot spot, creating chains of volcanoes (e.g., Hawaiian Islands).
  • Volcanic eruptions release gases (CO₂, SO₂, H₂O), ash, and lava—they can affect global climate.
